flashcards.back:
O(1)

Just update two pointers: newNode->next = head; head = newNode;

O(n)

Must traverse to find second-to-last node (can't go backward in singly linked list).

flashcards.front: Steps to Reverse a Linked List
flashcards.back:
1. Save next: next = curr->next
2. Reverse link: curr->next = prev
3. Move prev: prev = curr
4. Move curr: curr = next

Finally: head = prev

Use slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers.

If they meet → cycle exists
If fast reaches NULL → no cycle

Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps.

When fast reaches end, slow is at middle.

For even length, returns second middle.

1. Move first pointer n nodes ahead
2. Move both pointers together
3. When first reaches NULL, second is at nth from end

Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

flashcards.front: Key Edge Cases in Singly Linked List
flashcards.back:
1. Empty list (head == NULL)
2. Single node (head->next == NULL)
3. Two nodes (special handling for delete)
4. Position out of bounds

flashcards.front: Singly vs Doubly Linked List Trade-off
flashcards.back:
Singly: Less memory (one pointer), can only traverse forward, delete from end is O(n)

Doubly: More memory (two pointers), bidirectional traversal, delete from end is O(1)
